source: Rocky Linux Admin Guide, ch. 9.6.9-9.6.11 pgrep/pkill/killall/pstree (pp. 167-169)

questions.q: What does the pgrep command do?
options: Searches running processes by name and prints the matching PIDs
Sends a signal to processes by name
questions.why: pgrep finds processes by name and outputs their PIDs; pkill goes further and signals them.

questions.q: Find the PID(s) of the sshd process(es) owned by root.
questions.why: pgrep -u root sshd returns the PIDs of root's sshd processes.

questions.q: Send the default signal to all processes named tomcat (by name).
questions.why: pkill signals every process matching the name; killall tomcat does the same job.

questions.q: What signal do pkill and killall send by default?
questions.why: Both default to SIGTERM; you can specify another, e.g. pkill -9.

options: Ends the connection session attached to terminal pts/1
questions.why: pkill -t targets processes by terminal, so it can end a user's session on that tty.

questions.q: Kill all processes named tomcat using killall.
questions.why: killall <name> signals all matching processes (default SIGTERM); -i asks for confirmation, -l lists signal names.

questions.q: Display the process tree with each process's PID shown.
questions.why: pstree -p adds the PID to each process in the tree; -n sorts by PID, -u shows uid transitions.
